Sort of, it's a template sort of tale of two, side A, side B, just like the Wall Street Journal sales letter. 295 again shows attributing something to somebody rather than stealing it. 297 is my Weight Watcher CEO story which I sometimes tell, naming everybody, sometimes I tell, not naming everybody, but it's a pretty good story. 298 is Joe Carbo's I Was Poor Now I'm Rich story. 299 is just, it just shows you, this is from Bernie Goldberg's arrogance book, shows you a way to start right out with a story. So I'm sitting in a very nice conference room in the very nice time in life building high above the West 15th in Manhattan. And that's really kind of a good example of the unimportant detail. I mean really, what does it matter what street that building's on or what floor he's sitting on? Well it doesn't, but it does because if you strip out all that stuff you don't really have anything particularly interesting left. Page 300 is the transparency kind of story. 301 is a turning point kind of story. The best one of that that I have ever found anywhere on the planet is on 302 and it's Jim Rohn's Girl Scout at the Door story. Every time he uses it, I mean it moves people, it's a powerful story. He got that perfectly right. I have no idea if it ever happened or not, doesn't matter. But again, it's a template. I mean yours wouldn't be a Girl Scout, but you know you can find a way to take the details out and stick in your own details and it's a great template. 304 is Jim Rohn's mentor story. And Rohn's probably by the way as good at this as anybody. You know if you're going to listen to somebody's CDs for teaching persuasion and influence by first person storytelling, you can't go wrong with listening to Rohn's stuff. I gave you three here, but there's at least 90 of them that you know are really really good. 306 is his girlfriend and Carmel's single rose story, which nails every woman in the audience every time he tells it. And I have a few more that I didn't get copied, which I will. Rohn's got, if you don't have Rohn's little book by the way, Seven Strategies of Wealth, that's where I took these from. And there's about 10 more in here. So that's like a convenient resource because everything else he has is you know 5,000 CDs and 800 pages of stuff and it's a little overwhelming. And we referenced this once yesterday, but if you haven't read this book Tribal Knowledge, it's about Starbucks, but it's really not about Starbucks. So tribe, cult, following, use whatever word you want. It's a nice quick little simple book with some good principles in it. Okay, in a couple minutes I want to start working on your stuff. But first, our ending exercise of the day, wading through faxes and newsletters and so forth, who had some sort of epiphany, ah-ha, discovery, realization, identified something you're really not doing that you should be doing, validated something you're doing that, you know, like he said, it's kind of useful to see that I'm doing what you're doing. So who, Charlie? It dawned on me that it was, and as much as you're using your writing to influence you, you're using a bit of a pervy and you're putting in details, great stories, but really it's the power of story and the power of your own authenticity. You don't really have to make up a lot of stuff if you're living a pretty interesting life, which you do. The book that I wrote that I distributed pretty much meant most of this stuff because I have a lot of stories about me and my family, what I'm doing, why I'm doing what I think about. I had two book editors, two. Business book editors tell me to take those stories out, make them more covey-like, and I'm sitting on a manuscript the next book and going off to rewrite this thing only because of the stories that I have that are not there. The good news, I guess, of all of it is, is it's easier to write about what you know about so easily anyway. You, if you're doing it, once important. See, the interesting thing about, like, the professional editors is they can find a book like a Stephen Covey book and make the case, here's the best seller. But if you played the odds, I can show them 20 for everyone they can find, that may not have hit the New York Times list, but has been selling 50,000, 60,000, 30,000 copies a year for 40 years. So, you know, if you look at Zig Zee at the top, you go look at Think and Grow Rich, you go look at Maltz, you go look at almost Dale Carnegie's book, you look at any of these books that have outlived their authors and continue to influence years after there's no marketing, no advertising, no promotion, no nothing. They are fundamentally stories stitched together. That's what they are. And the other point about what you make, the point that you made, I think is important. Vance talks about a lot, is this word of authenticity, which I would link to the principle of transparency. You know, I mean, ultimately, first of all, people care generally more about who you are than what you've, certainly than what you've done, because we live in a country where people are pretty willing to forget about that in a short period of time. So they care more about who you are than what you've done than what you do or even what you offer them. And what they're really trying to figure out is, is this guy real? That really is at the core of all of this, people make their voting decisions, their buying decisions in any kind of personality driven thing by trying to decide is this guy real or is this guy faking it? And so stories, personal life experience stories are a way to get there. The other thing is the more diversity of them that you are willing to do. See, the more likely you are to have somebody connect with one of them and any one of them will do. So it's like what I told you about the juror on the Turner trial with the hair-lipped kid say, so like I get almost at every Peter Low event, I had somebody hang around afterwards to tell me about their kid who stutters. Now, there's only one line of disclosure in that entire speech about that. Most people of course didn't even notice it because they don't stutter, they don't have a kid that stutters, so it doesn't even register with them. But the person who does, that's like the only thing they heard and that's enough for them now. They want to bond. So everyone's got a bag in their hand where they went to the back and bought and now they want to tell me their kid story. And most of the mail is from the stories and it's so you knock down this person because they've had this problem, you knock down this person because they had this experience, you knock down this person. So if you had no dad growing up, there's a certain number of people who had no dad growing up. If you had two dads, there's a certain number of people who had two dads. If you grew up in a small town, there's people who grew up in a small town. You'd be amazed how much harness racing stuff I get and it's not like first generation. It's my grandfather used to go to the track every Saturday and he had this favorite horse or have you ever heard a so-and-so? Like you know there's three people in the whole business and we all know each other. But you know and so that those little points of connection, so the diversity of the stories is important. Because you knock, it's kind of like TV infomercials, mass media, you knock buyers off one at a time for all kinds of different reasons. It's not like they all buy for the same reason and it's not like they're all motivated at the same point and what they're really all looking for is this guy real.
